g.data-narration: Welcome to GPU Architecture and CUDA Programming. Let's explore how GPUs evolved into powerful parallel computing engines.
GPUs started as graphics accelerators but evolved into general-purpose parallel processors powering AI and scientific computing revolution.
CPUs optimize for latency with few complex cores, while GPUs optimize for throughput with thousands of simple cores executing in parallel.
GPU contains multiple Streaming Multiprocessors, each with many CUDA cores that execute threads in parallel with shared memory.
g.text: • Each SM contains 64-128 CUDA cores • Warp Scheduler manages 32-thread warps

g.data-narration: SIMT executes 32 threads as a warp in lockstep, each running the same instruction on different data for maximum efficiency.
g.text: • Warp divergence hurts performance • Threads in warp synchronized by default

g.data-narration: CUDA uses host-device model where CPU handles control flow and serial tasks while GPU executes parallel kernel functions.
g.text: • __global__ marks kernel functions • gridDim, blockDim, threadIdx identify threads

g.data-narration: Data flows between CPU host memory and GPU device memory via PCIe bus using cudaMemcpy for host-to-device and device-to-host transfers.
A CUDA kernel defines parallel computation where each thread calculates one output element using its thread index.
g.text: int i = threadIdx.x; // Unique thread ID
c[i] = a[i] + b[i]; // Each thread does 1 element

g.data-narration: Kernel launch configuration uses triple angle brackets specifying grid and block dimensions for parallel thread organization.
g.text: • Each block runs on one SM • Threads in block share memory and synchronize • Block IDs: blockIdx.x, Thread IDs: threadIdx.x

g.data-narration: GPUs excel at deep learning training, scientific simulations, molecular dynamics, and real-time graphics rendering.
g.text: • GPUs train neural networks 10-100x faster than CPUs • Molecular dynamics, weather modeling, fluid dynamics

g.data-narration: Key takeaway: GPUs use thousands of simple cores optimized for throughput while CPUs use few complex cores optimized for latency.
Use GPUs for large data-parallel problems with uniform operations. Use CPUs for complex control flow, small datasets, or irregular computations.
g.text: GPU vs CPU: When to Use Each
Large arrays • Uniform operations • High parallelism • Matrix/vector ops
Complex control flow • Small datasets • Irregular memory access • Serial tasks
Exam Tips: Write vectorAdd kernel and explain host-device data flow!
